summary: The post explains how to use the linker's --wrap flag to enable mocking in C, a testing technique common in higher-level languages but less straightforward in C. By passing -Wl,--wrap=function_name to the compiler, calls to that function are redirected to __wrap_function_name, allowing test code to substitute mock implementations. DeVault walks through a concrete example of wrapping fread and feof to test a file-reading function in isolation. He notes the limitation that only one wrapper per function is allowed per executable, and sketches out a vision for a future C test framework that would automate the wrapping process with a clean mocking API.
key_points: The linker --wrap flag redirects function calls to __wrap_ prefixed versions, enabling mock implementations without modifying source code
Both GNU Gold and LLVM's lld support --wrap, making it portable across major C toolchains
The technique also defines __real_ prefixed symbols for accessing the original functions when needed
A key limitation is that only one wrapper per function can exist in a single executable, requiring either multiple test binaries or smart configurable wrappers
DeVault envisions a C test framework that automates wrapper generation with a higher-level mocking API
money_quotes: One of the comforts I've grown used to in higher level languages when testing my code is mocking.
The idea is that in order to test some code in isolation, you should "mock" the behavior of things it depends on.
Using --wrap also conveniently defines __real_feof and __real_fread if we need them.
Unfortunately, you can't have two different wrappers for the same function in an executable.
Eventually I intend to write my own test framework for C, which will use wrappers to support mocking.
key_insight: The linker's --wrap flag provides a clean, non-invasive mechanism for mocking in C by redirecting function calls at link time, bringing a familiar testing pattern from higher-level languages to systems programming.
